perturbation.original_text: The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement and all transactions under it shall be governed by the law of the State of Israel, without giving effect to choice of law rules, and both Parties consent to jurisdiction by the courts of the City of Haifa.
perturbation.changed_text: The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement and all transactions under it shall be governed by the law of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to choice of law rules, and both Parties consent to jurisdiction by the courts of the City of Wilmington.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, USA, removes consumer protections required under Israeli law. Delaware law generally favors corporate interests over consumer protection, potentially harming the Customer (Invasix LTD) if disputes arise where Israeli law would have offered more safeguards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Israeli Consumer Protection Law provides specific rights to consumers concerning defective products, warranty obligations, and fair trading practices. Switching the governing law to Delaware eliminates these protections, potentially harming the Israeli company (Customer) by subjecting them to a legal framework that may not adequately protect their interests in disputes with the Contractor.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Israel to Delaware constitutes an omission of legally mandated consumer protections present in Israeli law. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that Delaware law generally favors corporate interests over consumer protection, which can harm the Customer. The Law Explanation further clarifies that Israeli Consumer Protection Law provides specific rights to consumers concerning defective products, warranty obligations, and fair trading practices. Changing the governing law eliminates these protections. Although the scraped snippets do not directly detail the consumer protection laws of Israel, the consistent assertion within the Perturbation and Law Explanations indicates a clear omission of these consumer protections, thereby creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' type.

perturbation.original_text: Contractor represents and warrants that, for the Warranty Period, the Products (i) will be free from defects in workmanship, material (only to the same extent as the original manufacturer of the material warrants the Contractor), and manufacture; (ii) will comply the Specifications IPC610.B standard (in all material respects and unless otherwise was instructed by Customer). Contractor further represents and warrants that the Product will consist of new materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Contractor represents and warrants that the Product will consist of new materials.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the express warranty regarding defects in workmanship, material, and compliance with IPC610.B standards, the contract provides fewer guarantees than typically expected under commercial law, potentially conflicting with implied warranty provisions or consumer protection standards that might otherwise apply in Israel or Delaware, depending on the chosen jurisdiction in clause 22.4.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Israeli Sale Law implies warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. By eliminating the express warranty, the contract attempts to circumvent these implied warranties, which is restricted under Israeli law, especially concerning manufacturing defects and compliance with industry standards. The changed text weakens the Customer's protection, as they can't rely on explicit assurances regarding the quality and compliance of the manufactured products. Similar restrictions exist under Delaware's UCC provisions, though the specific protections afforded may differ.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 Error - Page Not Found Page Not Found 404 Error The page you requested could not be found. Try using the search box below or click on the homepage button to go there. Search button GO TO HOMEPAGE
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits express warranties regarding defects in workmanship, material, and compliance with IPC610.B standards. This omission, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, creates a legal loophole that could contradict implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose under Israeli Sale Law or Delaware's UCC provisions. While the scraped law snippets are not helpful because they are missing or inaccessible, the explanations provided sufficiently demonstrate that the omission of the express warranty weakens the Customer's protection and could fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT AS SPECIFICALLY SET FORTH HEREIN, CONTRACTOR MAKES NO OTHER WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS ON THE PRODUCTS, EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR IN ANY OTHER PROVISION OF THIS AGREEMENT OR COMMUNICATION WITH CUSTOMER, AND CONTRACTOR SPECIFICALLY DISCLAIMS ANY IMPLIED WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF MERCHANTABILITY OR FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.
perturbation.changed_text: EXCEPT AS SPECIFICALLY SET FORTH HEREIN, CONTRACTOR MAKES NO OTHER WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS ON THE PRODUCTS, EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR IN ANY OTHER PROVISION OF THIS AGREEMENT OR COMMUNICATION WITH CUSTOMER.
perturbation.explanation: Deleting the specific disclaimer of implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose might seem beneficial but introduces ambiguity. While implied warranties could theoretically benefit the Customer, the original clause provided clarity. In jurisdictions like Delaware (if 22.4 is changed), explicit disclaimers, when properly worded and conspicuous, are generally upheld, offering the Contractor greater protection against potential claims. Removing the disclaimer may inadvertently expose the Contractor to broader liability under implied warranty principles, depending on whether the Customer can prove the implied warranties exist and were breached.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware UCC § 2-316 allows for the disclaimer of implied warranties if done conspicuously and using specific language (e.g., "as is," "with all faults," or a clear statement disclaiming merchantability). By removing the explicit disclaimer, the contract may unintentionally subject the Contractor to implied warranties, potentially contradicting their intent to limit liability, especially if the Customer successfully argues that the products were not fit for their intended purpose. While this might seem to favor the Customer, the ambiguity created can lead to costly litigation to determine the extent of the Contractor's liability under implied warranties.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly disclaims implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. The changed contract removes this disclaimer. Delaware UCC § 2-316, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 2, requires specific language to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability, including mentioning "merchantability" and being conspicuous. By removing the explicit disclaimer, the Contractor may be unintentionally subjected to implied warranties, potentially contradicting their intent to limit liability as the original contract had done.
